title: Lab 12.1: Capability Projection
summary: The system executes a latency-sensitive routing step successfully, but the decision remains buried inside cloud execution and cannot be inspected as a first-class proposal.
decision_question: What can a reader or tool actually discover before the system acts?

surfaces.name: edge projection
queries: list capabilities near the request entry point
show which inputs are locally available

surfaces.name: cloud catalog
queries: show policy authority for routing changes

proposal.summary: The cloud runtime shifts the routing step under load, but the reasoning is not emitted as an explicit proposal artifact.
assumptions: the latency budget is tight enough to justify movement
edge inputs appear sufficient for the route hint
unresolved: whether edge placement satisfies trust policy
whether the move remains auditable under compliance requirements

validation.summary: Validation exists inside the cloud path, but its reasoning is not queryable from outside execution.
authority_notes: authority is real, but invisible to the reader

execution.summary: Execution produces the right route selection result, but the final state cannot be traced back to a visible decision lifecycle.
placement: routing remains in the cloud because no explicit proposal was reviewed

trace.summary: The outcome exists, but no proposal, evaluation, or trace artifact explains how the system arrived there.

functions.planning: Planning is implicit inside the runtime path instead of being exposed as a machine-readable function output.
functions.validation: Validation happens internally, but the system does not separate it visibly from execution.
functions.execution: Execution both chooses and performs the change, which keeps the result correct but architecturally opaque.

expectations: Execution success is not enough if the system cannot expose what it knew and why it acted.
Partial discoverability starts with making the capability surface explicit, even before proposals become visible.
runtime_decisions: the browser request exposes enough context to imagine a better placement, but not enough to approve it
the cloud runtime still owns the outcome because no explicit proposal surface exists yet
